content:
# 1 John Chapter 4 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-06

---

### v.9 — "his only Son" vs. "his one and only Son"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "his only-begotten Son" | ton huion autou ton monogenē |
| LOGOS | "his only Son" | monogenēs = only, unique |
| PNEUMA | "his one and only Son" | Emphasis on uniqueness |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. monogenēs means "only" or "unique" — the term emphasizes singularity, not the mode of generation. EMET's "only-begotten" imports the later theological debate about gennēsis into a word that simply means "one of a kind." PNEUMA's "one and only" is redundant — "only" already means "one and only." LOGOS's "only Son" is precise and sufficient.

**Status:** Minor. Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

### v.10 — "propitiation" vs. "sacrifice to take away our sins"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "propitiation for our sins" | hilasmon peri tōn hamartiōn hēmōn |
| LOGOS | "the propitiation for our sins" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "a sacrifice to take away our sins" | Interprets hilasmos as expiation only |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. Same issue as 2:2 — hilasmos carries both propitiation (averting wrath) and expiation (removing sin). PNEUMA's "sacrifice to take away our sins" captures expiation but loses propitiation. Consistency with 2:2 requires the same rendering.

**Status:** ⚠️ Theologically significant. Consistent with 2:2 ruling. Propitiation vs. expiation remains the key issue.

---

### v.17 — "as he is so also are we in this world" vs. "we live like Jesus here in this world"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "just as that [one] is, so also are we in this world" | kathōs ekeinos estin kai hēmeis esmen en tō kosmō toutō |
| LOGOS | "as he is so also are we in this world" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "we live like Jesus here in this world" | "Live like Jesus" for "as he is, so are we" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The Greek says "as that one is (estin), so also are we (esmen)" — a statement of ontological likeness, not behavioral imitation. PNEUMA's "live like Jesus" makes it ethical ("imitate his lifestyle"), but the author's point is identity: we are in this world as he is — loved by the Father, confident in judgment, already sharing his nature. The present tenses are key: "as he is... so are we."

**Status:** ⚠️ Theologically significant. Ontological identity vs. ethical imitation.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Description |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA changes propitiation | 1 | "Sacrifice to take away sins" for hilasmos (v.10) |
| PNEUMA converts ontology to ethics | 1 | "Live like Jesus" for "as he is, so are we" (v.17) |
| PNEUMA adds emphasis | 1 | "One and only" for "only" (v.9) |

**Overall pattern:** Chapter 4's most significant disagreement is v.17 — whether our relationship to Christ in this world is ontological (we are as he is) or ethical (we live like him). The author's point is deeper than imitation: believers share Christ's standing before the Father, which is why they can have confidence on judgment day. The propitiation issue (v.10) continues the pattern from 2:2.

---

*— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA*
